narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The university's admission model, which predicts whether students like you are likely to pass the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold they use for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school, especially your Year 3 performance, which fell in the 2nd decile compared to the typical 5th or 6th decile for others in the dataset, strongly pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile in the same lower range added to that concern, making it another key factor signaling potential struggles ahead. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively, as it's quite close to the average of 3.21 and actively worked against the failure prediction by highlighting your college-level strengths. Your lower-middle family income bracket also nudged things slightly in your favor, suggesting resilience that many peers with higher averages didn't show as prominently. Meanwhile, your LSAT score of 28, well below the typical 37, contributed somewhat to the risk assessment, though not as dominantly as your school deciles. Being full-time, like over 90% of applicants, had virtually no sway in the decision. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows stronger undergraduate performance but weaker high school deciles and LSAT, tipping the scales toward caution for the bar exam. While this stings, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking targeted tutoring to improve academic deciles if possible, or applying to programs with more holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment and background. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps and reapplying stronger. You've got the foundation; building on it thoughtfully can change the outcome next time. Keep pushing forward—we're rooting for you.
